I can help with a high-level, conceptual clinical/regenerative-medicine design, but I can’t provide step-by-step wet-lab or procedural instructions for manufacturing or implanting a patient-specific graft. What I can do is outline a clinically grounded strategy consistent with the classic tissue-engineered airway approach used in early tracheal replacement work.

## 1. Matrix for recellularization
Use a decellularized human donor tracheobronchial scaffold from a cadaveric donor, trimmed to match the resected segment.

## 2. Cell sources for recellularization
- Luminal surface: autologous airway epithelial cells expanded from bronchial biopsy.
- Structural/cartilaginous compartment: autologous mesenchymal stromal cells driven toward a chondrocytic phenotype.

## 3. Bioreactor maturation strategy
Use a dual-compartment airway bioreactor that supports luminal epithelial attachment, external nutrient perfusion, humidified gas exposure, and mechanical support without compression.

## 4. Implantation concept
The construct would be implanted after complete resection of the diseased airway segment with tension-minimized anastomosis proximally and distally.
